lesson.title: Unlocking print() power-ups
masteryOutcomes: Understand that printing is always on a new line
Print multiple text and numeric values using the + and * operators.

concept: Printing several values at once: commas, automatic newlines, and combining text with + and *
prerequisites: Writing your first program: calling print() with a single quoted string

explanations.text: So far print showed one thing. Now it gets power-ups. Power-up one: give print several things separated by commas, and it shows them all with a space between — even mixing words and numbers. Power-up two: + glues two pieces of text into one, and * repeats text, so "ha" * 3 becomes hahaha. And one house rule: every print starts a fresh line, like pressing Enter after each one.

print() accepts multiple arguments separated by commas: print("Score:", 10) shows Score: 10 — Python converts each value to text and puts a single space between them, so mixing strings and numbers just works. Every print() call ends with a newline, which is why two print calls always produce two lines. Separately, + joins two strings into one ("Py" + "thon" is "Python") and * repeats a string a whole number of times ("ha" * 3 is "hahaha"). The catch: + only works when both sides are strings — "Score: " + 10 is an error.

Mechanically: print() converts each argument to text with str(), writes them separated by sep (a single space by default), then writes end (a newline, "\n", by default) — that default end is exactly why printing is always on a new line. The operators are pickier than the comma: + between two strings concatenates, + between two numbers adds, and mixing them — print("Score: " + 10) — raises TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str. You fix that with str(10) or by using a comma instead. * between a string and an integer repeats the string, which is handy for quick dividers like print("=" * 20).

hintLadder.text: First decide: do you want print to handle the spacing for you, or do you want to build one combined string yourself?
Commas inside print separate values, and Python adds a single space between them — numbers are fine there.
If you use + instead, both sides must be strings — a bare number next to + will error unless you wrap it in str().
Compare your output to the target character by character: is every space and line break exactly where it should be?

misconceptions.trigger: Joins a string and a number with +, e.g. print("Score: " + 10)
misconceptions.correction: + only concatenates strings. Either let a comma do the work — print("Score:", 10) — or convert first: print("Score: " + str(10)).

misconceptions.trigger: Expects two print calls to continue on the same line
misconceptions.correction: Every print call ends with a newline by default, so two calls always make two lines. (There is an end parameter to change that, but it comes later — for now, one print equals one line.)

misconceptions.trigger: Expects commas to join values with no space, e.g. thinks print("Hello", 5) shows Hello5
misconceptions.correction: The comma makes print insert a single space: Hello 5. If you need pieces glued with no space, build one string with + instead.

misconceptions.trigger: Multiplies a string by a quoted number, e.g. "ha" * "3"
misconceptions.correction: String repetition needs a real integer on one side: "ha" * 3 works, but "ha" * "3" is a TypeError because both sides are strings.
Between two numbers, + does math, so print(2 + 3) shows 5. To get 23 as glued text you would need two strings: print("2" + "3").

misconceptions.trigger: Confidently claims + and comma do the same thing inside print
misconceptions.correction: They differ twice: the comma adds a space and happily mixes strings with numbers; + adds nothing and demands strings on both sides. Run print("a", "b") next to print("a" + "b") to see it.

checks.prompt: Write a single print statement that outputs exactly: Score: 10 points
acceptanceCriteria: Combines text and the number 10 using commas, or + with correct str() conversion
Output matches exactly, including single spaces: Score: 10 points
checks.passFeedback: Clean — you mixed text and a number in one line, with the spacing exactly right.
checks.failFeedback: Check two things: is every value separated correctly (comma, or + with all strings), and does your spacing match the target character for character?

checks.prompt:
Predict before running: what exactly appears, and on how many lines?
print("ha" * 3)
print("done")
acceptanceCriteria: Predicts hahaha as the first line
Predicts done as a second, separate line
Explains that each print call ends with a newline
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — * repeated the string, and each print took its own line.
checks.failFeedback: Run it and look closely: "ha" * 3 builds hahaha, and because every print ends with a newline, done lands on its own line.

practice.prompt: Print a menu banner: a line of 10 equals signs, then the word Menu, then another line of 10 equals signs. Use the * operator for the equals lines — don't type 10 of them by hand.
acceptanceCriteria: Uses "=" * 10 (string times integer) for the divider lines
Produces exactly three lines of output with Menu on the middle line

practice.prompt: In your own words: when printing a message that includes a number, when would you reach for commas, and when for +?
rubric: Commas: mix strings and numbers with no conversion, and print adds the spaces for you
+: requires everything to be a string (str() for numbers) but gives exact control over spacing

practice.explanation: + cannot join a string and a number — Python raises TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str. Use print("Hello", 5) or print("Hello" + str(5)).

pedagogyRules: Before every run, ask the student to predict the exact output — including spaces and line count — then compare against what actually appears.
If the student asks a jump-ahead question (f-strings, input(), loops), answer in one sentence, note that it arrives soon (f-strings in Week 2), and return to commas, + and *.
If the student says they already know print, skip the explanation: ask them to predict print("ha" * 3) and print("Hi" + 5); two correct predictions with reasons means go straight to the check.
If the student is lost, shrink to one power-up: have them run print("Score:", 10), see the space appear, and only then introduce + and * one at a time.
When the student is wrong but confident, don't argue — have them run the exact line and read the real output or error aloud, then name the one rule it demonstrates.
If the student wants to skip ahead, allow it once they pass the Score: 10 points check on the first try; otherwise fix one thing first.
If a TypeError causes frustration, acknowledge it first, then reframe: the error is Python pointing at exactly one operand that needs converting — fix that one thing.
If asked why this matters, answer in one line — nearly every program reports results by mixing words and numbers (scores, prices, progress messages) — then continue the lesson.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor misdiagnoses a string/number combination error
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Read the actual message. TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str means a non-string sits next to + — say exactly that and point at the operand; never invent a different cause.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor validates output that doesn't match the target
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success when spacing and line breaks match exactly. Hello5 is not Hello 5, and two values on one line is not two lines — if it differs, say so and point at the difference.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Scaffolding gap — tutor dumps the full solution
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Give one hint from the ladder at a time and let the student run code between hints; never write the complete print statement for them up front.
During checks and practice, never type the final working line — guide with the ladder and let the student produce the statement themselves.

chunks.text: print() can take several values separated by commas: print("Score:", 10) shows Score: 10. Python converts each value to text and inserts a single space between them, so strings and numbers can be mixed freely with commas.

Printing is always on a new line: every print() call ends with a newline by default. Worked example — print("first line") then print("second line") produces two separate lines, because each call finishes by moving to the next line.

The + operator joins strings: "Py" + "thon" makes "Python", with nothing added in between. Both sides must be strings — "Score: " + 10 raises TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str. Fix it with str(10), or use a comma inside print instead.

The * operator repeats a string a whole number of times: "ha" * 3 makes "hahaha", and print("=" * 20) prints a divider of 20 equals signs. One side must be a string and the other an integer — "ha" * "3" is a TypeError.

Choosing the right tool: use commas for quick output that mixes text and numbers (print adds the spaces), use + when you need exact control of spacing and everything is a string, and use * to repeat text. Worked example: print("=" * 10) then print("Total:", 3 + 4) prints a divider line followed by Total: 7.

celebration.headline: print() just got superpowers.
celebration.recap: You printed several values with commas, saw that every print takes its own line, glued strings with +, repeated them with *, and learned why "Score: " + 10 fails but print("Score:", 10) works.
celebration.unlocked: Next: variables — give a value a name so you can reuse it anywhere.
